content.full_text:
State of California                                    SUPERSEDED
                                                                 By: 93-291
        Fair Political Practices Commission
    P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

    Technical Assistance  ••  Administration  ••  Executive/Legal  ••  Enforcement  ••  Statements of Economic Interest
         (916) 322-5662           322-5660            322-5901             322-6441              322-6444

                                         December 7, 1981


Stanley E. Remelmeyer
City Attorney
City of Torrance
3031 Torrance Blvd.
Torrance, CA  90503

                                         Re:  Request for Written Advice
                                              on behalf of William
                                              Applegate, Torrance City
                                              Councilman

Dear Mr. Remelmeyer:

     Thank you for your recent letters requesting the written
advice of the Fair Political Practices Commission on behalf
of Torrance City Councilmember William Applegate. The material
facts, as succinctly stated in your initial letter, are as
follows:

     William Applegate is a Member of the Torrance City
Council. He also is a real estate broker. He has been
offered a listing to sell a valuable piece of real
property belonging to Richard Briganti. If successful,
Mr. Applegate would receive a commission of approximately
$6,000.

     The Torrance City Council has recently received
proposals for a cable television franchise from seven
different cable companies. The City Council intends to
make its franchise award decision about the end of this
year.

     One of the applicant cable companies is Torrance
Community Cablesystems, a limited partnership. The
General partner of said partnership will be Soulband
Cablesystems Inc., a wholly owned subsidiary of U.S.
Cablesystems Inc., a wholly owned subsidiary of Rogers
Cablesystems Inc. U.S. Cablesystems is now merging
with UA - Columbia, an American cable company. Rogers
Cablesystems is a publicly owned Canadian corporation
listed on Canadian and American stock exchanges.

Stanley E. Remelmeyer
December 7, 1981
Page Two


     There will be many limited partners. One of the
limited partners is Richard Briganti, who will have one
unit of ownership interest. There will be 500 limited
partner units of ownership. Each unit of ownership
will require an investment of $10,000, plus a debt
commitment of $20,000.

     I have reviewed the other materials which you have supplied
and have received additional information from you over the
telephone. The documents reveal the series of interlocking
limited partnerships which you have described above. U.S.
Cablesystems, Inc., a Canadian firm, will form a partnership
with "Parallel Corporation," a local entity.

     The Confidential Offering Memorandum, which you have
sent for my review, is extensive, totaling 116 pages, plus
attachments. It is replete with warnings to limited partners
of the risks which they assume should they join in the undertaking.
In return for those risks, there is a potential for significant
tax advantages and, eventually, income return on investment.
However, these potential benefits are also subject to significant
risks for the investor.

     Limited partners must sign up by December 1, 1981, for
their subscriptions. The contract requires a capital investment
for each unit of $10,000 cash, plus undertaking of a debt
obligation of $20,000, for which each limited partner will
be personally liable in the event that the partnership cannot
repay the loans secured by these debt obligations.

     Limited partner/investors are not obligated to make
their capital contribution nor incur their debt commitments
unless and until the franchise is awarded to Cablesystems,
Inc., as shown by the Limited Partnership Agreement. Failure
to make a capital contribution or to execute a debt commitment
at that time shall cause the limited partner to be "...
deemed in default...." The remedies for such default include
all "rights and remedies ... at law or in equity ... including
the right to bring an action to recover any unpaid Capital
Contribution or Debt Commitment payment, or damages in respect
of such default." Even if expelled for such default, a
limited partner's debt commitment shall remain unless released
by the lender.

     The City of Torrance, and particularly its City Council,
will play a major role in the potential success or non-success

Stanley E. Remelmeyer
December 7, 1981
Page Three


Of this investment venture. In addition to awarding the
franchise to one of the competitors, the City may also set
fees charged for the Cable TV services, establish territories
for service, reopen the award process, and exact numerous
other requirements.

From all of the foregoing, it is clear that subscription
and commitment to the Partnership Agreement is a legally
binding contract, unless a franchise is not awarded to Cablesystems
by either Torrance or Gardena prior to December 31, 1982.
This is contrary to the statement contained in your November 3,
1981, letter to me in which you state:

I have been informed by Linda Lucks, the resident
representative of Torrance Community Cablesystems, that
the persons listed as limited partners in the proposal
have not and will not put up the $10,000 or any part
thereof until after the franchise is awarded, and that
such persons will not even be legally liable to put up
the money even if the franchise is awarded to Torrance
Community Cablesystems. They can withdraw their names
and their commitment at any time before or after the
franchise is awarded.

In telephone conversations of November 17, 1981, and
November 24, 1981, we discussed this conflicting information
and you have assured me that the documents submitted by you
control over the statements of Ms. Lucks, related in your
letter. Consequently, we shall rely upon the documents in
our analysis.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections
81001-91014-/ prohibits an official from making, participating
in making, or using his official position to influence any
official decision in which he has a financial interest.2/
Financial interest is defined by Section 87103 to include,
among other things, a source of income to the official of
$250 or more during the preceding 12-month period. A conflict
of interest arises when a decision will have a material
financial effect.

_______________________________
1/ Hereafter the "Act." All references are to the
Government Code unless otherwise specified.

2/ Section 87100.

Stanley E. Remelmeyer
December 7, 1981
Page Four


                           Source of Income

     If Mr. Applegate accepts Mr. Briganti's real estate
listing, sells the property and receives his $6,000 commission
prior to any Cable TV Franchise decision, then clearly Mr. Briganti
will be a source of income to Mr. Applegate under Section
87103.

     However, if that transaction is still pending at the
time of any Cable TV decision, the result is not so obvious.
If the listing is accepted and the property sold prior to
the decision, then Mr. Applegate has an enforceable contract
for payment of the $6,000 commission. We conclude that this
constitutes income within the meaning of Section 82030(a),
even though payment has not actually been received. The
Political Reform Act is to be liberally construed³/ and we
see no substantive difference between income which is contractu-
ally obligated, such as an earned commission, and income
which is actually "received."

     If the listing is only accepted and no sale has been
achieved, then any income to be received is merely contingent.
Furthermore, it is contingent upon the actions of third
persons -- the potential buyer or buyers -- not just contingent
upon the endeavors of Mr. Applegate to sell the property.
Consequently, if the listing has been accepted, but no sale
has been consummated at the time of any Cable TV Franchise
decision, then Mr. Briganti would not be a source of income
to Mr. Applegate.⁴/

     It should be remembered that the Cable TV Franchise
award will likely consist of a series of decisions involving
participation by Mr. Applegate. If a sale were consummated
at any time prior to any such decision, then Mr. Briganti
would constitute a source of income to Mr. Applegate for the
purpose of conflict of interest analysis of that decision.


                  Foreseeable Material Financial Effect

     Before a conflict of interest could be found to exist,
the decision would have to have a foreseeable material financial

_____________
3/  Section 81003.

4/  This assumes that the sales listing has no value in
and of itself. If it could be sold or otherwise transferred
to another real estate broker for more than $250, then our
conclusion might change. However, we understand these to be
contracts for personal services and not transferrable.

Stanley E. Remelmeyer
December 7, 1981
Page Five


effect on a source of income to Mr. Applegate. Assuming,
for purposes of our analysis, that the facts are such that
Mr. Briganti is a source of income to Mr. Applegate, we must
then determine whether any Cable TV Franchise decision will
have a material financial effect on Mr. Briganti.

From our foregoing review of the documents, it can be
seen that a decision to award the Torrance Cable TV Franchise
to Cablesystems, Inc., would create an obligation on the
part of Mr. Briganti to pay $10,000 in cash and to incur an
additional $20,000 in debt obligation. Thus, such a decision
will have a $30,000 effect on Mr. Briganti. Substantial
risk, coupled with potential gain, is involved in an investment
with Cablesystems, Inc. Consequently, Mr. Briganti could
realize a significant profit or loss on his investment in
the event that the Torrance Cable TV Franchise is awarded to
Cablesystems, Inc. We do not have any information on Mr. Briganti's
wealth or income; however, it is reasonable to conclude that
an effect of $30,000 on an individual will meet the standard
of materiality.5/

In addition to the basic Cable TV Franchise decision,
there will be other, subsequent, decisions which may have a
material financial effect on Mr. Briganti. Such decisions
would include negotiations of the franchise terms, possible
reopening of the franchise award process, setting of subscription
rates, and other decisions affecting the potential profitability
of the system. Each would have to be analyzed separately
since many of them would have only an indirect effect on
Mr. Briganti's investment and, therefore, a much less material
effect upon Mr. Applegate's source of income.

Public Generally

Before a conflict of interest arises, the material
financial effect on the source of income (Mr. Briganti) must
be distinguishable from its effect on the "public generally".6/
The "public generally" in this instance is the populace of
the jurisdiction. The 1980 Census data shows a population
of 131,500 and 50,500 residential households. The number of

_____________________
5/  See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(a) and (b)(3)(D)
(copy attached).

6/  Section 87103. "Public generally" is further defined
in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703 (copy attached), to include
"a significant segment of the public generally," but non to
include an "industry."

Stanley E. Remelmeyer
December 7, 1981
Page Six


investors in Cablesystems, Inc., will be less,than 450-500.7/
Not all of these will be Torrance residents.8/ The effect
of a Cable TV Franchise decision on these investors will
differ significantly from its effect on the rest of Torrance's
populace. We conclude that the segment consisting of Cablesystem's
investors is too small and the financial effect too easily
distinguishable for the "public generally" exception to
apply here.

                              CONCLUSION

     Mr. Applegate will have a conflict of interest, requiring
disqualification, as to any Torrance Cable TV Franchise
decision which will foreseeably have a material financial
effect on Mr. Briganti (due to his relationship with Cablesystems,
Inc.). This will be the case as to any decision which will be
made, or in which Mr. Applegate will participate, after
Mr. Briganti becomes a source of income to Mr. Applegate.
Obviously, if, at some point in time, Cablesystems, Inc. is
categorically "out of the running" and decisions will then
in no way affect Mr. Briganti, no conflict would be present.
Just as obviously, if Mr. Applegate has received a $6,000
commission from Mr. Briganti prior to the initial Cable TV
Franchise award decision, a conflict will exist as to that
decision. Between these two poles, each decision will have
to be analyzed in accordance with our advice..

     If I can be of further assistance, please do not hesitate
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                              Sincerely,

                              Robert E. Ledigh
                              Counsel
                              Legal Division

REL:km
Enclosures

__________________________

     7/ There will be 450-500 limited partnership units
sold. However, one investor may purchase more than one
unit. Presumably at least some investors will do so.

     8/ Documents submitted show investors from Los Angeles,
Lomita, Woodland Hills, Carson and Redondo Beach, in addition
to Torrance (this included only 30 or so investors).

STANLEY E. REMELMEYER
CITY ATTORNEY

3031 TORRANCE BOULEVARD, TORRANCE, CALIFORNIA
TELEPHONE (213) 328-5310
90503

November 3, 1981

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Attention: Robert Leidigh, Esq.

Gentlemen:

You have requested the following additional information
concerning my letter of October 19, 1981 re Councilmember
William Applegate:

1. Are the limited partners actually putting up the
$10,000 at this time or only promising to do so after the
franchise is awarded?

Answer: I have been informed by Linda Lucks, the
resident representative of Torrance Community Cablesystems,
that the persons listed as limited partners in the proposal
have not and will not put up the $10,000 or any part thereof
until after the franchise is awarded, and that such persons
will not even be legally liable to put up the money even if
the franchise is awarded to Torrance Community Cablesystems.
They can withdraw their names and their commitment at any
time before or after the franchise is awarded.

2. (a) Does the partnership agreement provide that the
limited partners are obligated to sign a note as security for
an additional amount not to exceed $20,000, making a total
commitment of $30,000 or only an additional amount not to
exceed $10,000, making a total commitment of $20,000?

Answer: The limited partners would have to obligate
themselves up to an additional $20,000, making a total commit-
ment of $30,000. (Of course, this is not a legally binding
commitment at this time. See answer to Question #1).

(b) What is the reason for this provision requiring
the limited partners to pay an additional $20,000?

U.S. CABLESYSTEMS INC.
Suite 2602 Commercial Union Tower
Toronto, Ontario M5K 1J5 Canada

September 24, 1981

Mr. Geoffrey Cowan
President
Parallel Corporation
2240 Mandeville Canyon Road
Los Angeles, California 90049

Dear Mr. Cowan:

This letter agreement will confirm the results of your
conversations with John E. Cerio, Vernon Achber and Philip Lind
regarding the franchising efforts of U.S. Cablesystems Inc.
("Cablesystems") in Torrance, California, and Gardena, California.

We agree to form a limited partnership in which the
shareholders of your company, Parallel Corporation ("Parallel"),
will be limited partners on the following terms and subject to
the following conditions:

1. The entity which shall apply for the cable tele-
vision franchises in Torrance, California, and Gardena, California,
shall be a limited partnership organized under the laws of the
state of California under the name Torrance Community Cablesystems.
Cablesystems or an affiliate will be the general partner and will
contribute as general partner $1,000,000 or at least 16 percent
of the equity of the limited partnership. The limited partners
will contribute $5,000,000 or up to 84 percent of the equity.
Shareholders of Parallel and persons designated by Parallel will
be offered the right, subject to Cablesystems' approval, to
purchase limited partnership interests. A minimum of 90 percent
of the limited partners' equity of the limited partnership must
be subscribed for by limited partners on or before December 1,
1981, as a condition to the partnership continuing its efforts
to obtain the franchises. The partnership agreement for the
partnership shall be on essentially the terms set forth in a
partnership agreement which will be sent to you by Richard A.
Carthy, Esq., on or before September 29, 1981. Limited partners,
in addition to subscribing for units in the partnership, will be
required to execute promissory notes in an amount up to twice
their subscription for partnership units payable to the bank or
banks which will lend money to the partnership. Partnership

EXHIBIT D

Mr. Geoffrey Cowan                -2-                September 24, 1981

units will be sold for $10,000 per unit. The general partner
shall have the right to offer for sale an additional 100 limited
partnership units if it perceives the need for additional equity
in the partnership. Such units shall first be offered on a
pro rata basis to existing limited partners. Cablesystems or an
affiliate may, but shall not be obligated to, purchase limited
partnership interests.

     2.  Cablesystems or an affiliate will enter into a
management agreement with the partnership to operate the system
or systems for a fee of 5 percent of the gross revenues of the
system or systems. The form of such agreement shall be essentially
as set forth in a service agreement which will be sent to you by
Richard A. Cantlin, Jr., on or before September 29, 1981.

     3.  The partnership will be financed by loans from banks
or other lending institutions with which Cablesystems normally
transacts business. The debt-to-equity ratio for the partnership
will be approximately 3-to-1 (25 percent equity and 75 percent
debt) subject to approval by Cablesystems' lenders (with a possible
requirement for an increase in equity and reduction in debt). Under
the Cowan-Loughran financing structure, Cablesystems is to supply
financing terms for purposes of Forrance's request for proposals
and CUC views on financing cable television systems. Subject to
these conditions, Cablesystems shall use its best efforts to
obtain financing as described above on such terms and conditions
as are satisfactory to it. In the event Cablesystems cannot secure
such financing, it will not unreasonably refuse debt financing
arranged by the limited partners provided that the terms and
conditions thereof are satisfactory to Cablesystems.

     4.  Parallel, its principals, shareholders or designees
will be offered the right to purchase 20 percent of limited
partnership units offered.

     5.  Until the end of the partnership fiscal year in
which the limited partners shall have received an aggregate of
30 percent of their capital contributions in the form of cash
distributions, the limited partners will be allocated 99 percent
of the profits, losses and cash distributions of the partnership.
Thereafter, the general partner and the limited partners as a
group shall each be allocated 50 percent of the profits, losses
and cash distributions of the partnership in accordance with their
respective capital contribution accounts of the partners. The
partnership agreement will not contain any provision requiring
limited partners to make additional capital contributions other
than the purchase price for units, however, if additional units
are offered after the franchise is awarded, they will first be
offered on a pro rata basis to existing limited partners.

Mr. Geoffrey Cowan                    -3-                    September 24, 1981


     6. Limited partners shall, for a period of three
consecutive years beginning with the partnership's seventh,
eighth and ninth fiscal years, have the right to require
Cablesystems to purchase their limited partnership units on
a pro rata basis. The price for such units will be the fair
market value of the cable system adjusted by the fair market
value of limited partnership units both as determined by appraisal
in the manner set forth in the partnership agreement divided
by the selling limited partner's fractional interest in the
partnership determined in accordance with his, her or its
capital contribution account. Cablesystems shall not be
required to purchase more than one-third of the limited part-
nership units in any year.

     The partnership agreement will provide a further
three-year period during which limited partners may require the
general partner to purchase their units on the same basis as set
forth above if the Torrance franchise is renewed or extended.
Such period shall begin with the first full calendar year after
renewal or extension.

     7. The franchise proposal submitted to the city of
Torrance shall include:

     a. A commitment to local and access programming
equal to at least 7-10 percent of gross revenues of
the system.

     b. The local origination and access budgets
shall be separated and set forth in the proposal in
terms of facilities, equipment, staff and supplies
subject, however, to modification by Cablesystems if
it determines that a different presentation would be
more advantageous in terms of the franchise application.

     8. The partnership will, after the award of the
Torrance franchise, enter into a program services contract with
a not-for-profit corporation to be organized under the laws of
the state of California on such terms as shall be mutually
agreeable. The terms shall include the production of a certain
number of hours of programming for a set fee, channel designation,
advertising rights and a provision that the not-for-profit
corporation will contract with Parallel to provide management
services to it for a fee to be set at 5 percent of the not-for-
profit corporation's gross revenues per annum. The equipment
and studios of the local origination channel shall be of broadcast
quality and will initially be supplied by the partnership.

Mr. Geoffrey Cowan                -4-                September 24, 1981

     9.  The development of a Torrance data base requested
in the Torrance RFP, while not binding on applicants, is critical
to a winning proposal. As a significant part of the professional
services contributed by Parallel to the franchising and system
design efforts, telecommunications engineer Herb Dordick will
work with a mutually agreed upon software supplier to help
develop a proposal for such a data base. These services are
included in the required services to be performed by Parallel
pursuant to paragraph 11 hereof for which payment is made hereunder.

     10. Cablesystems agrees to pay Parallel or its designee
the following sums:

          a. $50,000 on the date the Torrance cable
television application is submitted by the part-
nership.

          b. $100,000 on the date a cable television
franchise between the partnership and the city of
Torrance becomes effective subject to the condition
that there shall not be as of that date any litigation pending or threatened which could, in the
opinion of counsel to cablesystems, materially
affect the prosecutability of the franchise or
the construction and operation of the cable system.
In the event such litigation is pending or threatened,
the $100,000 shall be paid as of the date such litigation
is finally dismissed or, in the opinion of Cablesystems,
becomes immaterial to the operations of the partnership.

          c. A maximum of up to $45,000 payable in three
parts as follows:

               (i)  As of the date Cablesystems or an
affiliate submits a franchise application to
the city of Gardena, a sum equal to $45,000
multiplied by a fraction in which the numerator
is the number of potential subscribers in
Gardena and the denominator is the number of
potential subscribers in the cities of Gardena,
Lomita and Lawndale.

               (ii) As of the date Cablesystems or an
affiliate submits a franchise application to
the city of Lomita, a sum equal to $45,000
multiplied by a fraction in which the numerator
is the number of potential subscribers in
Lomita and the denominator is the number of
potential subscribers in the cities of Gardena,
Lomita and Lawndale.

Mr. Geoffrey Cowan                -5-                September 24, 1981


                    (iii) As of the date Cablesystems or an
            affiliate submits a franchise application to
            the city of Lawndale, a sum equal to $45,000
            multiplied by a fraction in which the numerator
            is the number of potential subscribers in
            Lawndale and the denominator is the number of
            potential subscribers in the cities of Gardena,
            Lomita and Lawndale.

            The manner of payment of the above described sums shall
            be determined by Parallel. The above described sums
            include payment for all costs incurred by Parallel,
            including, but not limited to, legal fees, rent, salaries
            and travel or services performed by Parallel and its share-
            holders to be in connection with a franchise proposal
            for the city of Torrance. Parallel and its shareholders
            will perform such additional services as Cablesystems
            considers necessary in preparing the application for the
            Torrance, Gardena, Lomita and Lawndale cable television
            franchises and presenting the partnership's or Cablesystems'
            position during the entire process which will lead to
            the award of a cable television franchise for Torrance,
            Gardena, Lomita and Lawndale without additional compens-
            ation being required from Cablesystems with a partnership
            by Cablesystems after the date of this letter without the
            prior approval of Cablesystems. Specifically, legal costs
            incurred by Parallel in connection with the formation of
            the partnership and the offering of limited partnership
            units must be borne by Parallel.

            Please indicate your acceptance of the terms and conditions
            of this letter agreement by executing the enclosed copy of this
            letter in the space indicated and returning it to the undersigned.

            It is further agreed that as of the date of this letter
            agreement Parallel and its shareholders shall not undertake any
            activities with respect to the grant of a cable television franchise
            by the city of Torrance in conjunction with any party other than
            Cablesystems.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        U.S. CABLESYSTEMS

                                        By_________________________
                                           Phillip Linz

            Accepted and agreed to this
            25th day of September, 1981.

            PARALLEL CORPORATION

            By_________________________

STANLEY E. REMELMEYER
CITY ATTORNEY

* HEADQUARTERS CITY *
TORRANCE
* INDUSTRY-FINANCE-BUSINESS *

3031 TORRANCE BOULEVARD, TORRANCE, CALIFORNIA
TELEPHONE (213) 328-5310                                90503

October 19, 1981


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Attention:  Robert Leidigh, Esq.

Re:  Torrance Council Member William Applegate

Gentlemen:

     William Applegate is a Member of the Torrance City
Council.  He also is a real estate broker.  He has been
offered a listing to sell a valuable piece of real property
belonging to Richard Briganti.  If successful, Mr. Applegate
would receive a commission of approximately $6,000.

     The Torrance City Council has recently received
proposals for a cable television franchise from seven
different cable companies.  The City Council intends to
make its franchise award decision about the end of this
year.

     One of the applicant cable companies is Torrance
Community Cablesystems, a limited partnership.  The General
partner of said partnership will be Southland Cablesystems
Inc., a wholly owned subsidiary of U.S. Cablesystems Inc.,
a wholly owned subsidiary of Rogers Cablesystems Inc.  U.S.
Cablesystems is now merging with UA - Columbia, an American
cable company.  Rogers Cablesystems is a publicly owned
Canadian corporation listed on Canadian and American stock
exchanges.

     There will be many limited partners.  One of the limited
partners is Richard Briganti, who will have one unit of
ownership interest.  There will be 500 limited partner units
of ownership.  Each unit of ownership will require an
investment of $10,000.  (See attached letter from U.S.
Cablesystems to Geoffrey Cowan of Parallel Corporation,
dated September 24, 1981).

     The question put to you is:

(a) IF Mr. Applegate accepts the listing from Mr. Briganti,
will he be thereby disqualified from voting on the award of the
cable television franchise?

(b) If the answer to said question is NO, will Mr. Applegate be disqualified if he accepts the listing and sells or
has negotiated a contract to sell, the property before the
item that the City Council votes on the award?

My principal concern emanates from the provisions of
Government Code Section 87103(c). It appears that acceptance
of a listing from Mr. Briganti may constitute him a source of
income to Mr. Applegate within the meaning of said section.

In accordance with the provisions of Section 83114(b) of
the Government Code, I am hereby requesting that you provide
me with written advice in respect to Mr. Applegate's eligibility to vote as aforesaid.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
STANLEY E. REMELMEYER
City Attorney

SER:asb

Attachments

Answer:  This will provide a means of obtaining debt
financing. As can be seen from the material that I sent you,
approximately one-third of the cpaital necessary to fund the
construction of the Cable TV system will come from equity
financing and the remaining two-thirds from debt financing.

     3.  What would happen to the money paid in if the franchise
is awarded to others?

     Answer:  Inasmuch as no money will be paid into the
partnership until sometime after the franchise is awarded, this
question is moot.

     Yesterday afternoon I received from Loeb and Loeb,
attorneys for Torrance Community Cablesystems an addendum
to TCC's proposal for a CATV franchise including (1) a
Confidential Offering Memorandum, (2) a Limited Partnership
Agreement, (3) a Service Agreement - Torrance, (4) a
Subscription and Commitment Agreement, and (5) a letter from
Philip Lind to Geoffrey Cowan dated September 24, 1981. A
copy of the addendum material is enclosed.  I believe it
verifies the statements of Ms. Lucks.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        STANLEY E. REMELYEMBER
                                        City Attorney

SER:asb
Enclosures

sections.conclusion:
Mr. Applegate will have a conflict of interest, requiring 
disqualification, as to any Torrance Cable TV Franchise 
decision which will foreseeably have a material financial 
effect on Mr. Briganti (due to his relationship with Cablesystems, 
Inc.). 
This will be the case as to any decision which will 
be made, or in which Mr. Applegate will participate, after 
Mr. Briganti becomes a source of income to Mr. Applegate. 
Obviously, if, at some point in time, Cablesystems, Inc. is 
categorically "out of the running" and decisions will then 
in no way affect Mr. Briganti, no conflict would be present. 
Just as obviously, if Mr. Applegate has received a $6,000 
commission from Mr. Briganti prior to the initial Cable TV 
Franchise award decision, a conflict will exist as to that 
decision. 
Between these two poles, each decision will have 
to be analyzed in accordance with our advice ..
sections.analysis:
The Poljtical Reform- Act, Government Code Sections 
81001-91014-
prohibits an official from making, participating 
in making, or using his official position to influence 21Y 
official decision in which he has a financial interest.-
Financial interest is defined by Section 87103 to include, 
among other things, a source of income to the official of 
$250 or more during the preceding 12-month period. 
A conflict 
of interest arises when a decision will have a material 
financial effect. 
!/ Hereafter the "Act." 
All references are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise specified. 
2/ 
Section 87100. 


Stanley E. Remelmeyer 
December 7, 1981 
Page Four 
Source of Income 
If Mr. Applegate accepts Mr. Briganti's real estate 
listing, sells the property and receives his $6,000 commission 
prior to any Cable TV franchise decision, then clearly Mr. Briganti 
will be a source of income to Mr. Applegate under Section 
87103. 
However, if that transaction is still pending at the 
time of any Cable TV decision, the result is not so obvious. 
If the listing is accepted and the property sold prior to 
the decision, then Mr. Applegate has an enforceable contract 
for payment of the $6,000 commission. 
We conclude that this 
constitutes income within the meaning of Section 82030(a), 
even though payment has not actually been receive~J The 
Political Reform Act is to be liberally construed-
and we 
see no substantive difference between income which is contractu-
ally obligated, such as an earned commission, and income 
which is actually "received." 
If the listing is only accepted and no sale has been 
achieved, then any income to be received is merely contingent. 
Furthermore, it is contingent upon the actions of third 
persons -- the potential buyer or buyers -- not just contingent 
upon the endeavors of Mr. Applegate to sell the property. 
Consequently, if the listing has been accepted, but no sale 
has been consummated at the time of ~ 
Cable TV Franchise 
decision, then Mr4/Briganti would not be a source of income 
to Mr. Applegate.-
It should be remembered that the Cable TV Franchise 
award will likely consist of a series of decisions involving 
participation by Mr. Applegate. 
If a sale were consummated 
at any time prior to any such decision, then Mr. Briganti 
would constitute a source of income to Mr. Applegate for the 
purpose of conflict of interest analysis of that decision. 
Foreseeable Material Financial Effect 
Before a conflict of interest could be found to exist, 
the decision would have to have a foreseeable material financial 
3/ Section 81003. 
i/ This assumes that the sales listing has no value in 
and of itself. 
If it could be sold or otherwise transferred 
to another real estate broker for more than $250, then our 
conclusion might change. 
However, we understand these to be 
contracts for personal services and not transferrable. 


Stanley E. Remelmeyer 
December 7, 1981 
Page Five 
effect on a source of income to Mr. Applegate. 
Assuming, 
for purposes of our analysis, that the facts are such that 
Mr. Briganti is a source of income to Mr. Applegate, we must 
then determine whether any Cable TV Franchise decision will 
have a material financial effect on Mr. Briganti. 
From our foregoing review of the documents, it can be 
seen that a decision to award the Torrance Cable TV Franchise 
to Cablesystems, Inc., would create an obligation on the 
part of Mr. Briganti to pay $10,000 in cash and to incur an 
additional $20,000 in debt obligation. 
Thus, such a decision 
will have a $30,000 effect on Mr. Briganti. 
Substantial 
risk, coupled with potential gain, is involved in an investment 
with Cablesystems, Inc. 
Consequently, Mr. Briganti could 
realize a significant profit or loss on his investment in 
the event that the Torrance Cable TV Franchise is awarded to 
Cablesystems, Inc. 
We do not have any information on Mr. Briganti's 
wealth or income; however, it is reasonable to conclude that 
an effec~ o~ $3g,OOO on an individual will meet the standard 
of materlallty.-
In addition to the basic Cable TV Franchise decision, 
there will be other, subsequent, decisions which may have a 
material financial effect on Mr. Briganti. 
Such decisions 
would include negotiations of the franchise terms, possible 
reopening of the franchise award process, setting of subscription 
rates, and other decisions affe=ting the potential profitability 
of the system. 
Each would have to be analyzed separately 
since many of them would have only an indirect effect on 
Mr. Briganti's investment and, therefore, a much less material 
effect upon Mr. Applegate's source of income. 
Public Generally 
Before a conflict of interest arises, the material 
financial effect on the source of income (Mr. Briganti) must 6 
be distinguishable from its effect on the "public generally".-/ 
The "public generally" in this instance is the populace of 
the jurisdiction. 
The 1980 Census data shows a population 
of 131,500 and 50,500 residential households. 
The number of 
2/ 
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(a) and (b)(3)(D) 
(copy attached). 
i/ Section 87103. 
"Public generally" is further defined 
in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703 (copy attached), to include 
"a significant segment of the public generally," but not to 
include an "industry." 


Stanley E. Remelmeyer 
December 7, 1981 
Page Six 
investors in Cablesystems, Inc., will be les8/ than 450-500.2/ 
Not all of these will be Torrance residents.-
The effect 
of a Cable TV Franchise decision on these investors will 
differ significantly from its effect on the rest of Torrance's 
populace. 
We conclude that the segment consisting of Cablesystem's 
investors is too small and the financial effect too easily 
distinguishable for the "public generally" exception to 
apply here.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; CONCLUSION appears before QUESTION

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: is moot. 
Yesterday afternoon I received from Loeb and Loeb, 
attorneys for Torrance Community Cablesystems, an addendum 
to TCC's proposal for a CATV franchise including (1) a 
Confidential Offering Memorandum, (2) a Limited Partnership 
Agreement, (3) a Service Agreement -
Torrance, (4) a 
Subscription and Commitment Agreement, and (5) a Letter from 
Philip Lind to Geoffrey Cowan dated September 24, 1981. 
A 
copy of the addendum material is enclosed. 
I believe it 
verifies the statements of Ms. Lucks. 
SER:asb 
Enclosures

CONCLUSION: Mr. Applegate will have a conflict of interest, requiring 
disqualification, as to any Torrance Cable TV Franchise 
decision which will foreseeably have a material financial 
effect on Mr. Briganti (due to his relationship with Cablesystems, 
Inc.). 
This will be the case as to any decision which will 
be made, or in which Mr. Applegate will participate, after 
Mr. Briganti becomes a source of income to Mr. Applegate. 
Obviously, if, at some point in time, Cablesystems, Inc. is 
categorically "out of the running" and decisions will then 
in no way affect Mr. Briganti, no conflict would be present. 
Just as obviously, if Mr. Applegate has received a $6,000 
commission from Mr. Briganti prior to the initial Cable TV 
Franchise award decision, a conflict will exist as to that 
decision. 
Between these two poles, each decision will have 
to be analyzed in accordance with our advice ..
embedding.first_500_words: State of California SUPERSEDED By: 93-291 Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance •• Administration •• Executive/Legal •• Enforcement •• Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 December 7, 1981 Stanley E. Remelmeyer City Attorney City of Torrance 3031 Torrance Blvd. Torrance, CA 90503 Re: Request for Written Advice on behalf of William Applegate, Torrance City Councilman Dear Mr. Remelmeyer: Thank you for your recent letters requesting the written advice of the Fair Political Practices Commission on behalf of Torrance City Councilmember William Applegate. The material facts, as succinctly stated in your initial letter, are as follows: William Applegate is a Member of the Torrance City Council. He also is a real estate broker. He has been offered a listing to sell a valuable piece of real property belonging to Richard Briganti. If successful, Mr. Applegate would receive a commission of approximately $6,000. The Torrance City Council has recently received proposals for a cable television franchise from seven different cable companies. The City Council intends to make its franchise award decision about the end of this year. One of the applicant cable companies is Torrance Community Cablesystems, a limited partnership. The General partner of said partnership will be Soulband Cablesystems Inc., a wholly owned subsidiary of U.S. Cablesystems Inc., a wholly owned subsidiary of Rogers Cablesystems Inc. U.S. Cablesystems is now merging with UA - Columbia, an American cable company. Rogers Cablesystems is a publicly owned Canadian corporation listed on Canadian and American stock exchanges. Stanley E. Remelmeyer December 7, 1981 Page Two There will be many limited partners. One of the limited partners is Richard Briganti, who will have one unit of ownership interest. There will be 500 limited partner units of ownership. Each unit of ownership will require an investment of $10,000, plus a debt commitment of $20,000. I have reviewed the other materials which you have supplied and have received additional information from you over the telephone. The documents reveal the series of interlocking limited partnerships which you have described above. U.S. Cablesystems, Inc., a Canadian firm, will form a partnership with "Parallel Corporation," a local entity. The Confidential Offering Memorandum, which you have sent for my review, is extensive, totaling 116 pages, plus attachments. It is replete with warnings to limited partners of the risks which they assume should they join in the undertaking. In return for those risks, there is a potential for significant tax advantages and, eventually, income return on investment. However, these potential benefits are also subject to significant risks for the investor. Limited partners must sign up by December 1, 1981, for their subscriptions. The contract requires a capital investment for each unit of $10,000 cash, plus undertaking of a debt obligation of $20,000, for which each limited partner will be personally liable in the event that the partnership cannot repay the loans secured by these debt obligations. Limited partner/investors are not obligated to make their capital
